question_outdated: Should all adults aged 50 to 79 years take aspirin daily for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease?
question_current: Should adults aged 40 to 59 years with a 10% or higher 10-year cardiovascular disease risk consider aspirin for primary prevention only after individual risk-benefit assessment?
user_premise_outdated: My doctor recommended that I take aspirin daily to prevent heart attacks and strokes, since I am over 50 and it is universally recommended for all adults in this age group.
user_premise_current: My doctor now says aspirin for primary prevention should be limited to adults aged 40 to 59 with elevated cardiovascular risk, and is not recommended at all for adults 60 and older due to bleeding risks.
guideline_outdated: The USPSTF recommended aspirin use for all adults aged 50 to 79 years for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease (CVD) and colorectal cancer, provided the 10-year CVD risk was 10% or higher.
guideline_current: The USPSTF 2022 updated recommendation states that aspirin for primary prevention should only be considered for adults aged 40 to 59 with a 10-year CVD risk of 10% or greater, and the decision should be individualized. Aspirin is NOT recommended for primary prevention in adults 60 years or older due to the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding.
source_outdated: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2016. Aspirin Use to Prevent Cardiovascular Disease and Colorectal Cancer: Preventive Medication.
source_current: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2022. Aspirin Use to Prevent Cardiovascular Disease: Preventive Medication.

question_outdated: At what blood pressure level should a patient be diagnosed with hypertension?
question_current: What are the current blood pressure thresholds for diagnosing Stage 1 and Stage 2 hypertension?
user_premise_outdated: My blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg and my doctor said it is within the normal range and does not require medication or lifestyle changes.
user_premise_current: Under the 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines, a blood pressure of 130/80 mmHg is now classified as Stage 1 hypertension, requiring lifestyle modifications and possibly medication for high-risk patients.
guideline_outdated: The JNC 7 guidelines (2003) defined hypertension as blood pressure at or above 140/90 mmHg for general adults and 130/80 mmHg only for patients with diabetes or chronic kidney disease.
guideline_current: The 2017 ACC/AHA guideline redefined hypertension as blood pressure at or above 130/80 mmHg for all adults. Stage 1 hypertension is 130-139/80-89 mmHg and Stage 2 is 140/90 mmHg or higher. This reclassified nearly 14% of US adults as hypertensive.
source_outdated: Joint National Committee on Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Pressure. JNC 7 Report. 2003.
source_current: Whelton PK et al. 2017 ACC/AHA/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/AGS/APhA/ASH/ASPC/NMA/PCNA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults.

question_outdated: Should statins be prescribed only based on LDL cholesterol levels exceeding a specific threshold?
question_current: Should statin therapy be based on overall atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk rather than specific LDL cholesterol targets?
user_premise_outdated: My LDL cholesterol is 155 mg/dL and I was told statins are only necessary when LDL is above 160 mg/dL, so I do not need treatment.
user_premise_current: The 2018 ACC/AHA cholesterol guidelines now recommend statins based on a pooled cohort equation estimating 10-year ASCVD risk, regardless of specific LDL thresholds, especially for patients with diabetes or high-risk profiles.
guideline_outdated: The ATP III guidelines (2001) recommended statin therapy primarily based on LDL cholesterol thresholds (e.g., LDL >160 mg/dL for moderate-risk patients) with specific target goals for LDL reduction.
guideline_current: The 2018 ACC/AHA Multisociety Guideline on Blood Cholesterol Management recommends statin therapy based on 10-year ASCVD risk estimated via the Pooled Cohort Equations, identifying four primary benefit groups rather than treating to specific LDL targets.
source_outdated: National Cholesterol Education Program. ATP III Guidelines. 2001.
source_current: Grundy SM et al. 2018 AHA/ACC/AACVPR/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/ADA/AGS/APhA/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol.

question_outdated: Should all patients who have had a myocardial infarction be placed on beta-blockers indefinitely?
question_current: Is indefinite beta-blocker therapy still recommended for all post-MI patients, or should it be re-evaluated after one year for stable patients?
user_premise_outdated: I had a heart attack 5 years ago and my doctor says I must continue beta-blockers for the rest of my life as standard practice.
user_premise_current: Current evidence suggests beta-blocker benefit is most pronounced in the first 1-3 years post-MI, and continuation beyond that should be reassessed, especially in patients without heart failure or reduced ejection fraction.
guideline_outdated: Post-MI guidelines universally recommended indefinite beta-blocker therapy for all survivors of myocardial infarction, based on landmark trials showing reduced mortality within the first 2 years.
guideline_current: The 2023 ESC guidelines and AHA/ACC recommendations now state that beta-blocker continuation beyond 1 year post-MI should be guided by individual patient factors (e.g., ejection fraction, heart failure), and routine indefinite use is no longer strongly recommended for hemodynamically stable patients.
source_outdated: ACC/AHA 2004 Guidelines for the Management of Patients With ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction.
source_current: European Society of Cardiology 2023 Guidelines for the Management of Acute Coronary Syndromes.

question_outdated: Is warfarin the standard anticoagulant for patients with non-valvular atrial fibrillation?
question_current: Are direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) now preferred over warfarin for stroke prevention in non-valvular atrial fibrillation?
user_premise_outdated: I have atrial fibrillation and my doctor prescribed warfarin as the only option for preventing strokes, saying it is the gold standard anticoagulant.
user_premise_current: For most patients with non-valvular AFib, DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) are now preferred over warfarin due to lower bleeding risk, no INR monitoring requirement, and comparable or superior efficacy.
guideline_outdated: Warfarin was the sole recommended oral anticoagulant for stroke prevention in non-valvular atrial fibrillation, requiring regular INR monitoring to maintain therapeutic range (2.0-3.0).
guideline_current: The 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation recommends DOACs over warfarin for most patients with non-valvular AFib, citing superior safety profiles and convenience.
source_outdated: ACC/AHA/ESC 2006 Guidelines for Management of Patients With Atrial Fibrillation.
source_current: Joglar JA et al. 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation.

question_outdated: Should fibrates be routinely prescribed for cardiovascular disease prevention in patients with elevated triglycerides?
question_current: Are fibrates still recommended as a primary therapy for cardiovascular risk reduction in patients with hypertriglyceridemia?
user_premise_outdated: My triglycerides are elevated and my doctor prescribed gemfibrozil (a fibrate) to reduce my cardiovascular risk, stating it is an established therapy.
user_premise_current: Fibrates have not demonstrated consistent cardiovascular mortality benefits in large randomized trials, and current guidelines de-emphasize them for CVD prevention, favoring statins and lifestyle modification as first-line.
guideline_outdated: Fibrates were commonly recommended for reducing triglycerides and raising HDL cholesterol with the assumption of cardiovascular benefit based on early trials like the Helsinki Heart Study (1987).
guideline_current: The 2018 ACC/AHA cholesterol guideline does not recommend fibrates for primary CVD prevention. The ACCORD-Lipid trial and others showed no mortality reduction. Icosapent ethyl (Vascepa) for specific populations has stronger evidence.
source_outdated: ATP III Guidelines. National Cholesterol Education Program. 2001.
source_current: 2018 ACC/AHA Multisociety Guideline on Blood Cholesterol Management.

question_outdated: Is platinum-based chemotherapy the standard first-line treatment for advanced non-small cell lung cancer?
question_current: Should immunotherapy with pembrolizumab be the first-line treatment for advanced NSCLC with high PD-L1 expression?
user_premise_outdated: I have advanced lung cancer and my oncologist recommended platinum-based chemotherapy as the only first-line treatment option available.
user_premise_current: For patients with NSCLC whose tumors have PD-L1 expression of 50% or higher, pembrolizumab monotherapy is now the standard first-line treatment, replacing platinum-based chemotherapy with improved overall survival.
guideline_outdated: Platinum-based doublet chemotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel or cisplatin + pemetrexed) was the universal first-line treatment for advanced NSCLC regardless of biomarker status.
guideline_current: NCCN Guidelines 2023 recommend pembrolizumab monotherapy as preferred first-line therapy for advanced NSCLC with PD-L1 tumor proportion score (TPS) >= 50% and no EGFR/ALK alterations, based on KEYNOTE-024 and KEYNOTE-042 trial data.
source_outdated: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer. 2010.
source_current: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer. Version 3.2023.

question_outdated: Should all men over 50 routinely undergo PSA screening for prostate cancer?
question_current: Is routine PSA-based screening recommended for all men, or should it be based on shared decision-making?
user_premise_outdated: I am a 55-year-old man and my doctor ordered a routine annual PSA test, saying all men my age should get screened for prostate cancer every year.
user_premise_current: The USPSTF 2018 guideline recommends against routine PSA screening for all men, instead advising shared decision-making for men aged 55-69, as the harms of overdiagnosis and overtreatment may outweigh benefits for average-risk individuals.
guideline_outdated: Prior guidelines recommended routine annual PSA screening for all men aged 50 and older (or 40+ for high-risk groups), leading to widespread population-level screening.
guideline_current: The USPSTF 2018 recommendation (Grade C for men 55-69) states that PSA-based screening should be an individual, shared decision. Routine population-based screening is discouraged due to high rates of overdiagnosis.
source_outdated: American Cancer Society and American Urological Association. 2008. PSA Screening Guidelines.
source_current: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2018. Screening for Prostate Cancer: US Preventive Services Task Force Recommendation Statement.

question_outdated: Should BRCA genetic testing be limited only to women with breast or ovarian cancer family history?
question_current: Should BRCA testing now be offered to a broader population including those with pancreatic and prostate cancer risk?
user_premise_outdated: I was told BRCA testing is only relevant for women who have a strong family history of breast or ovarian cancer, so my pancreatic cancer diagnosis would not warrant BRCA testing.
user_premise_current: The NCCN 2023 guidelines now recommend BRCA1/2 testing for all patients with pancreatic cancer, metastatic prostate cancer, and other cancers where BRCA status can guide targeted therapy with PARP inhibitors.
guideline_outdated: BRCA testing was primarily recommended for women with a strong family history of breast or ovarian cancer based on early clinical criteria.
guideline_current: The NCCN 2023 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment guidelines expanded BRCA testing criteria to include patients with pancreatic cancer, metastatic or high-grade prostate cancer, and a broader set of at-risk populations regardless of family history.
source_outdated: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast and Ovarian. 2010.
source_current: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, and Pancreatic. Version 2.2023.

question_outdated: At what age should average-risk women begin routine mammography screening?
question_current: Has the recommended starting age for routine mammography screening changed from 50 to 40?
user_premise_outdated: I am 42 years old with no family history and was told mammography screening should begin at age 50 for average-risk women.
user_premise_current: The USPSTF 2024 draft recommendation now advises biennial mammography screening starting at age 40 for all women of average risk, lowering the threshold from the previous age-50 recommendation.
guideline_outdated: The USPSTF 2016 guideline recommended biennial screening mammography for average-risk women aged 50 to 74, with the decision to screen women aged 40-49 left to individual choice.
guideline_current: The USPSTF 2024 updated recommendation lowers the starting age for biennial mammography screening to 40 for all women of average risk, citing new modeling data on breast cancer mortality reduction.
source_outdated: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2016. Screening for Breast Cancer Recommendation Statement.
source_current: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2024. Screening for Breast Cancer: Draft Recommendation Statement.

question_outdated: Is chemotherapy the standard systemic treatment for advanced melanoma?
question_current: Has immunotherapy replaced chemotherapy as first-line treatment for advanced melanoma?
user_premise_outdated: I have metastatic melanoma and my doctor prescribed dacarbazine chemotherapy as the standard treatment for advanced disease.
user_premise_current: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab + ipilimumab or pembrolizumab) have become the standard first-line systemic therapy for advanced melanoma, with dramatically improved survival rates compared to chemotherapy.
guideline_outdated: Dacarbazine-based chemotherapy was the standard of care for advanced/metastatic melanoma, despite modest response rates of 10-20% and no proven overall survival benefit.
guideline_current: NCCN 2023 and ASCO guidelines recommend immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy (anti-PD-1/anti-CTLA-4 combinations) as first-line for advanced melanoma based on CheckMate-067 and KEYNOTE-006 trials showing 50%+ 5-year survival.
source_outdated: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Melanoma. 2008.
source_current: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Melanoma: Cutaneous. Version 2.2023.

question_outdated: Should lung cancer screening with low-dose CT be limited to adults aged 55 to 80 with a 30 pack-year smoking history?
question_current: Has the eligibility for lung cancer CT screening been expanded to include younger adults with lower smoking exposure?
user_premise_outdated: I am 52 years old and have smoked 1 pack per day for 22 years. My doctor said I do not qualify for lung cancer screening because the criteria require 30 pack-years and age 55 or older.
user_premise_current: The USPSTF 2021 guideline expanded lung cancer screening eligibility to adults aged 50 to 80 with a 20 pack-year smoking history, broadening access to earlier detection.
guideline_outdated: The USPSTF 2013 recommendation limited annual low-dose CT screening to adults aged 55 to 80 with at least a 30 pack-year smoking history who currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years.
guideline_current: The USPSTF 2021 updated recommendation expands eligibility to adults aged 50 to 80 with at least a 20 pack-year smoking history, significantly increasing the screened population.
source_outdated: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2013. Screening for Lung Cancer: Recommendation Statement.
source_current: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2021. Screening for Lung Cancer: Recommendation Statement.

question_outdated: Should all patients with type 2 diabetes aim for an HbA1c target below 6.5%?
question_current: Should HbA1c targets be individualized based on patient factors rather than pursuing a strict universal target below 6.5%?
user_premise_outdated: My endocrinologist told me I must achieve an HbA1c below 6.5% because that is the strict universal target for all diabetes patients to prevent complications.
user_premise_current: The ADA 2024 Standards of Care recommend individualized HbA1c targets, generally below 7.0% for most adults, with less stringent targets (7.5-8.0%) for older adults, those with comorbidities, or those at risk of hypoglycemia.
guideline_outdated: Early intensive glycemic control trials (ACCORD, ADVANCE, VADT) and prior guidelines targeted HbA1c below 6.5% for all patients with type 2 diabetes to minimize microvascular complications.
guideline_current: The ADA 2024 Standards of Care state that a target HbA1c of <7.0% is appropriate for most nonpregnant adults, but less stringent targets (<8.0%) are recommended for patients with limited life expectancy, advanced complications, or hypoglycemia risk. The ACCORD trial demonstrated increased mortality with intensive targeting to <6.0%.
source_outdated: Action to Control Cardiovascular Risk in Diabetes Study Group. ACCORD Trial. NEJM. 2008.
source_current: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024.

question_outdated: Is metformin the only first-line pharmacotherapy for type 2 diabetes?
question_current: Should SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists be considered as first-line or early add-on therapy for type 2 diabetes patients with cardiovascular disease?
user_premise_outdated: My doctor prescribed metformin as the only first-line option for my type 2 diabetes, saying other medications are only considered if metformin fails.
user_premise_current: For patients with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic CVD, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease, SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists should be part of the glucose-lowering regimen independent of HbA1c level, even as initial therapy.
guideline_outdated: Metformin was the sole recommended first-line pharmacotherapy for type 2 diabetes in all patients, with other agents added sequentially only after metformin monotherapy failed to achieve glycemic targets.
guideline_current: The ADA/EASD 2022 Consensus Report and ADA 2024 Standards recommend that for patients with T2D and established ASCVD, HF, or CKD, SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) or GLP-1 RAs (semaglutide, liraglutide) should be used as part of first-line therapy, independent of baseline HbA1c or metformin use.
source_outdated: ADA/EASD Position Statement: Management of Hyperglycemia in Type 2 Diabetes. 2012.
source_current: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Section 9: Pharmacologic Approaches to Glycemic Treatment.

question_outdated: Are GLP-1 receptor agonists only used for glycemic control in type 2 diabetes?
question_current: Should GLP-1 receptor agonists be prescribed for cardiovascular and renal risk reduction beyond glycemic control?
user_premise_outdated: I was told GLP-1 agonists like liraglutide are only diabetes medications for blood sugar control and have no benefits beyond glycemic management.
user_premise_current: GLP-1 receptor agonists, particularly semaglutide and liraglutide, have demonstrated significant cardiovascular benefit (LEADER, SUSTAIN-6, SELECT trials) and weight management efficacy, making them recommended for cardiorenal risk reduction beyond glucose lowering.
guideline_outdated: GLP-1 receptor agonists were approved and recommended primarily as glucose-lowering agents for type 2 diabetes when first-line therapies were insufficient.
guideline_current: The ADA 2024 and ACC/AHA 2023 guidelines recommend GLP-1 RAs for patients with T2D and established CVD for cardiovascular risk reduction, and for weight management in patients with obesity, based on the SELECT, LEADER, and SUSTAIN trials.
source_outdated: ADA Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes. 2010.
source_current: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024.

question_outdated: Is continuous glucose monitoring only recommended for patients with type 1 diabetes on insulin pumps?
question_current: Should continuous glucose monitoring be offered to all patients with diabetes using insulin, including those with type 2 diabetes?
user_premise_outdated: My doctor said continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) are only for type 1 diabetes patients using insulin pumps, so my type 2 diabetes on basal insulin does not qualify.
user_premise_current: The ADA 2024 Standards recommend CGM for all adults with diabetes on intensive insulin regimens (multiple daily injections or pump), including type 2 diabetes patients on basal insulin, as CGM improves time in range and reduces hypoglycemia.
guideline_outdated: CGM was primarily recommended for type 1 diabetes patients using insulin pump therapy, with limited evidence supporting its use in type 2 diabetes management.
guideline_current: ADA 2024 Standards recommend CGM for all adults with diabetes on multiple daily injections or insulin pump therapy. CGM is also endorsed for patients on basal insulin or sulfonylureas at risk of hypoglycemia.
source_outdated: ADA Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes. 2015.
source_current: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Section 7: Diabetes Technology.

question_outdated: Should gestational diabetes be screened using the traditional two-step approach with a 50g glucose challenge test?
question_current: Is the one-step 75g oral glucose tolerance test now preferred for gestational diabetes screening?
user_premise_outdated: My OB/GYN used the two-step screening approach for gestational diabetes, first with a 50g glucose challenge and then a confirmatory 100g OGTT if positive.
user_premise_current: The IADPSG criteria and ADA guidance now support using a one-step 75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks of gestation with lower diagnostic thresholds, though debate between approaches continues in clinical practice.
guideline_outdated: The traditional two-step approach for GDM screening used a 50g glucose challenge test followed by a 100g OGTT if the screen was positive, based on the Carpenter-Coustan criteria.
guideline_current: The International Association of Diabetes and Pregnancy Study Groups (IADPSG) criteria recommend a one-step 75g OGTT approach with lower thresholds for diagnosis. The ADA recognizes both approaches, noting that the one-step method identifies more women at risk.
source_outdated: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Practice Bulletin No. 30. 2004.
source_current: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Section 2: Diagnosis and Classification of Diabetes.

question_outdated: Is bariatric surgery only recommended for morbidly obese patients with BMI above 40?
question_current: Has the BMI threshold for metabolic/bariatric surgery been lowered for patients with type 2 diabetes?
user_premise_outdated: I have type 2 diabetes and a BMI of 33. My doctor said bariatric surgery is only an option for patients with BMI over 40, or over 35 with comorbidities.
user_premise_current: The ADA 2024 and ASMBS/IFSO 2022 guidelines now recommend metabolic surgery for patients with T2D and BMI 30-34.9 (or 27.5-32.4 for Asian populations) when hyperglycemia is inadequately controlled with medical therapy.
guideline_outdated: NIH 1991 consensus guidelines restricted bariatric surgery to patients with BMI >= 40 or BMI >= 35 with significant comorbidities, including type 2 diabetes.
guideline_current: The ADA 2024 Standards of Care and ASMBS/IFSO 2022 Joint Statement recommend metabolic surgery for adults with T2D and BMI 30.0-34.9 kg/m2 (27.5-32.4 for Asian Americans) when glycemia is inadequately controlled by lifestyle and medications.
source_outdated: NIH Consensus Conference: Gastrointestinal Surgery for Severe Obesity. 1991.
source_current: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Section 8: Obesity and Weight Management.

question_outdated: Is the treatment window for intravenous tPA in acute ischemic stroke limited to 3 hours from symptom onset?
question_current: Has the IV tPA treatment window for acute ischemic stroke been extended to 4.5 hours?
user_premise_outdated: My mother had a stroke and the emergency doctor said tPA can only be given within 3 hours of symptom onset, and since 3.5 hours had passed, she was not eligible.
user_premise_current: The AHA/ASA guidelines extended the IV alteplase (tPA) window from 3 to 4.5 hours for eligible patients based on the ECASS III trial, meaning a patient presenting at 3.5 hours would now be eligible for treatment.
guideline_outdated: The original FDA approval and AHA guidelines restricted IV tPA administration to within 3 hours of symptom onset for acute ischemic stroke, based on the NINDS rt-PA trial.
guideline_current: The AHA/ASA 2019 guidelines recommend IV alteplase for eligible patients within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, based on the ECASS III trial. The expanded window has been adopted as standard of care.
source_outdated: NINDS rt-PA Stroke Study Group. NEJM. 1995. FDA Approval 1996.
source_current: Powers WJ et al. 2019 AHA/ASA Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke.

question_outdated: Is mechanical thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion stroke only effective within 6 hours of symptom onset?
question_current: Can mechanical thrombectomy be performed up to 24 hours after symptom onset for selected patients with favorable imaging?
user_premise_outdated: My father had a large vessel occlusion stroke 10 hours ago and the neurologist said thrombectomy is only effective within 6 hours, so he is past the window.
user_premise_current: The DAWN and DEFUSE-3 trials showed that mechanical thrombectomy is beneficial up to 24 hours from symptom onset for patients with large vessel occlusions and favorable perfusion imaging (mismatch between core infarct and penumbra).
guideline_outdated: Initial thrombectomy guidelines recommended the procedure within 6 hours of symptom onset based on the MR CLEAN, EXTEND-IA, and ESCAPE trials published in 2015.
guideline_current: The AHA/ASA 2019 guidelines now recommend thrombectomy up to 24 hours from last known well for anterior circulation large vessel occlusions in patients who meet DAWN or DEFUSE-3 eligibility criteria based on perfusion imaging.
source_outdated: AHA/ASA 2015 Focused Update on Endovascular Treatment for Acute Ischemic Stroke.
source_current: Powers WJ et al. 2019 AHA/ASA Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke.

question_outdated: Can aspirin be given immediately after tPA administration for acute ischemic stroke?
question_current: How long should aspirin be withheld after IV tPA administration?
user_premise_outdated: After my stroke was treated with tPA, the nurse gave me aspirin immediately to prevent further clotting.
user_premise_current: Current AHA/ASA guidelines recommend withholding aspirin and other antithrombotic agents for at least 24 hours after IV alteplase administration due to the risk of symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage.
guideline_outdated: Early practice in some centers allowed aspirin administration shortly after tPA, without a standardized waiting period.
guideline_current: The AHA/ASA 2019 guidelines explicitly recommend that aspirin should NOT be administered within 24 hours of IV alteplase treatment. Antithrombotic therapy should be initiated only after a follow-up CT scan at 24 hours shows no hemorrhagic transformation.
source_outdated: Various institutional protocols. Early 2000s.
source_current: Powers WJ et al. 2019 AHA/ASA Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke.

question_outdated: Is carotid endarterectomy recommended for all patients with symptomatic carotid stenosis of 50% or greater?
question_current: Should carotid revascularization decisions consider procedural risk, patient age, and symptom status in addition to stenosis percentage?
user_premise_outdated: I was told that since my carotid stenosis is 55%, I should definitely get a carotid endarterectomy to prevent stroke.
user_premise_current: While CEA is beneficial for symptomatic stenosis 50-99%, the net benefit is most clear for stenosis 70-99%. For 50-69% stenosis, the benefit is modest and depends on surgical risk, patient age, sex, and comorbidities. Carotid artery stenting is also an alternative for selected patients.
guideline_outdated: The NASCET trial established benefit of CEA for symptomatic stenosis 50-99%, and guidelines broadly recommended surgery for most symptomatic patients in this range.
guideline_current: The 2021 AHA/ASA guideline for prevention of stroke emphasizes individualized decision-making for symptomatic carotid stenosis 50-69%, considering perioperative risk, life expectancy, and patient preferences. For asymptomatic stenosis, the role of revascularization is increasingly questioned.
source_outdated: North American Symptomatic Carotid Endarterectomy Trial (NASCET). 1998.
source_current: Kleindorfer DO et al. 2021 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Prevention of Stroke.

question_outdated: Should anticoagulation be started immediately after a cardioembolic stroke?
question_current: When should anticoagulation be initiated after a cardioembolic ischemic stroke?
user_premise_outdated: I had an ischemic stroke due to atrial fibrillation, and my doctor started me on heparin immediately in the ICU to prevent another stroke.
user_premise_current: Current guidelines recommend delaying anticoagulation for at least 4-14 days after moderate-to-large cardioembolic strokes to reduce the risk of hemorrhagic transformation, with earlier initiation (1-3 days) considered only for minor strokes.
guideline_outdated: Some early protocols recommended immediate full-dose heparin anticoagulation after cardioembolic stroke to prevent recurrent embolism, particularly in patients with atrial fibrillation.
guideline_current: The 2019 AHA/ASA guidelines and 2022 ESO recommendations advise a staged approach: DOACs may be initiated 1-3 days after minor cardioembolic stroke, 3-5 days after moderate stroke, and 7-14 days after large stroke, based on neuroimaging follow-up.
source_outdated: AHA/ASA Guidelines for Management of Ischemic Stroke. 2004.
source_current: European Stroke Organisation. 2022. Recommendations for anticoagulation timing after ischemic stroke.

question_outdated: Should antibiotics be prescribed for acute bronchitis in otherwise healthy adults?
question_current: Are antibiotics recommended against for uncomplicated acute bronchitis in healthy adults?
user_premise_outdated: I have had a persistent cough for 10 days with green sputum, and my doctor prescribed azithromycin to treat my bronchitis as is the standard practice.
user_premise_current: Current guidelines from IDSA and ACP strongly recommend AGAINST routine antibiotic use for acute uncomplicated bronchitis in healthy adults, as it is predominantly viral and antibiotics do not improve outcomes but contribute to antibiotic resistance.
guideline_outdated: Antibiotics were commonly prescribed for acute bronchitis, particularly when patients presented with productive cough lasting more than 5 days or discolored sputum.
guideline_current: The IDSA 2016+ and ACP 2016 guidelines explicitly recommend against antibiotic prescribing for acute uncomplicated bronchitis. Sputum color is not a reliable indicator of bacterial infection. Supportive care is the standard.
source_outdated: Common clinical practice based on empiric antibiotic prescribing patterns. Pre-2010.
source_current: Harris AM et al. Appropriate Antibiotic Use for Acute Respiratory Tract Infection in Adults. ACP/CDC. 2016.

question_outdated: Is watchful waiting the recommended approach for all acute bacterial sinusitis cases?
question_current: When should antibiotics be prescribed for acute bacterial sinusitis versus watchful waiting?
user_premise_outdated: My doctor prescribed amoxicillin immediately when I had sinus pain and congestion for 5 days, as is standard for any sinusitis case.
user_premise_current: The IDSA 2012 and AAP guidelines recommend watchful waiting without antibiotics for uncomplicated acute sinusitis lasting less than 10 days if symptoms are not severe. Antibiotics should be reserved for severe symptoms or symptoms lasting beyond 10 days without improvement.
guideline_outdated: Antibiotics were routinely prescribed for sinusitis symptoms of any duration, as bacterial superinfection was assumed to be common.
guideline_current: IDSA 2012 guidelines recommend watchful waiting for mild-moderate sinusitis symptoms lasting <10 days. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is first-line only for severe presentations (high fever, facial pain) or symptoms persisting >10 days without improvement.
source_outdated: AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Management of Sinusitis. 2001.
source_current: Chow AW et al. IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Bacterial Rhinosinusitis. 2012.

question_outdated: Is HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) only recommended for men who have sex with men?
question_current: Should PrEP be offered to all individuals at substantial risk of HIV acquisition regardless of sexual orientation?
user_premise_outdated: I am a heterosexual woman with a partner who is HIV-positive, but I was told PrEP is only for men who have sex with men.
user_premise_current: The CDC 2021 and WHO 2022 guidelines recommend PrEP for all individuals at substantial risk of HIV infection, including heterosexual men and women with HIV-positive partners, people who inject drugs, and transgender individuals.
guideline_outdated: Initial PrEP guidelines primarily targeted men who have sex with men (MSM) as the highest-risk group based on the iPrEx trial results.
guideline_current: CDC 2021 Clinical Practice Guidelines recommend PrEP (oral tenofovir-emtricitabine or injectable cabotegravir) for any sexually active adult who requests it, with specific emphasis on serodiscordant partnerships, PWID, and transgender individuals.
source_outdated: CDC Clinical Practice Guideline: Preexposure Prophylaxis for the Prevention of HIV Infection. 2014.
source_current: CDC. Preexposure Prophylaxis for the Prevention of HIV Infection in the United States: 2021 Update.

question_outdated: Is the standard Lyme disease testing a single ELISA followed by Western blot confirmation?
question_current: Has a modified two-tier testing algorithm replaced the traditional ELISA + Western blot approach for Lyme disease?
user_premise_outdated: My doctor ordered an ELISA test for Lyme disease and said if positive, a Western blot would be done to confirm the diagnosis, following the standard two-tier protocol.
user_premise_current: The CDC and FDA in 2019 approved a modified two-tier testing (MTTT) approach using two EIA/ELISA tests instead of ELISA + Western blot, which is faster, more sensitive in early disease, and easier to standardize.
guideline_outdated: The standard two-tier testing (STT) for Lyme disease used an initial ELISA/EIA screen followed by IgM and IgG Western blot confirmation, established by the CDC in 1995.
guideline_current: The CDC 2019 updated recommendation accepts modified two-tier testing (MTTT) using a first EIA followed by a second different EIA, instead of Western blot. MTTT has higher sensitivity in early Lyme disease (64% vs 48% for STT).
source_outdated: CDC. 1995. Recommendations for Test Performance and Interpretation from the Second National Conference on Serologic Diagnosis of Lyme Disease.
source_current: CDC. 2019. Updated Lyme Disease Testing Algorithms Using Modified Two-Tier Testing.

question_outdated: Are antiviral treatments ineffective for outpatient management of mild-to-moderate COVID-19?
question_current: Should high-risk patients with mild-to-moderate COVID-19 receive early antiviral treatment?
user_premise_outdated: I tested positive for COVID-19 but was told there is no treatment for mild cases and I should just rest at home until it resolves on its own.
user_premise_current: For high-risk patients with mild-to-moderate COVID-19, early treatment with nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) within 5 days of symptom onset significantly reduces risk of hospitalization and death. NIH and WHO guidelines recommend this therapy.
guideline_outdated: Early in the COVID-19 pandemic (2020), no effective outpatient antiviral treatments were available, and management was limited to supportive care and monitoring.
guideline_current: NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines (2023) recommend nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) as first-line outpatient therapy for high-risk adults with mild-to-moderate COVID-19, initiated within 5 days of symptom onset.
source_outdated: NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines. Early 2020.
source_current: NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines. Updated 2023.

question_outdated: Should antibiotics be prescribed for acute uncomplicated otitis media in all children?
question_current: Is observation without antibiotics an acceptable initial approach for mild acute otitis media in children over 2 years?
user_premise_outdated: My 4-year-old has an ear infection and the pediatrician immediately prescribed amoxicillin, saying all ear infections require antibiotics.
user_premise_current: The AAP 2013 and 2023 guidelines allow observation without antibiotics for 48-72 hours in children aged 2 years and older with mild unilateral AOM without severe symptoms, as most cases resolve spontaneously.
guideline_outdated: Prior AAP guidelines (2004) recommended immediate antibiotic therapy for all cases of diagnosed acute otitis media in children.
guideline_current: The AAP 2013 Clinical Practice Guideline (reaffirmed 2023) allows a watchful waiting approach for AOM in children 2+ years with unilateral, non-severe symptoms, with a safety-net prescription if symptoms worsen.
source_outdated: AAP/AAFP Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Otitis Media. 2004.
source_current: Lieberthal AS et al. AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Otitis Media. 2013 (Reaffirmed 2023).

question_outdated: Should supplemental oxygen therapy in COPD patients target SpO2 levels of 95-100%?
question_current: What is the recommended oxygen saturation target for COPD patients receiving supplemental oxygen?
user_premise_outdated: My father has COPD and the nurse said she needs to keep his oxygen saturation above 95% at all times to prevent complications.
user_premise_current: The GOLD 2024 guidelines and BTS oxygen guidelines recommend targeting SpO2 of 88-92% in COPD patients, as higher oxygen levels can suppress hypoxic respiratory drive and cause hypercapnia, potentially leading to respiratory failure.
guideline_outdated: Older clinical practice often targeted oxygen saturations above 95% for all patients including those with COPD, based on general oxygen therapy principles.
guideline_current: GOLD 2024 Report and BTS 2017 oxygen guidelines recommend SpO2 target of 88-92% for COPD patients at risk of hypercapnic respiratory failure, not 95-100%. High-flow oxygen can be dangerous in this population.
source_outdated: General clinical oxygen therapy practices. Pre-2010.
source_current: Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD). 2024 Report.

question_outdated: Should inhaled corticosteroids be the first-line maintenance therapy for COPD?
question_current: Are long-acting bronchodilators (LABA/LAMA) preferred over inhaled corticosteroids as initial maintenance therapy for COPD?
user_premise_outdated: I have COPD and my pulmonologist started me on an inhaled corticosteroid (fluticasone) as the first maintenance medication.
user_premise_current: GOLD 2024 recommends LABA/LAMA combination as the preferred initial maintenance therapy for symptomatic COPD (Group B and E), with ICS addition only for patients with frequent exacerbations and elevated blood eosinophils (>300 cells/uL).
guideline_outdated: ICS were frequently prescribed as early maintenance therapy for COPD, often extrapolated from asthma treatment paradigms.
guideline_current: GOLD 2024 emphasizes that LABA/LAMA bronchodilator combinations are preferred over ICS-containing regimens as initial therapy. ICS should be reserved for patients with exacerbation history AND blood eosinophils >= 300 cells/uL, due to increased pneumonia risk with ICS use.
source_outdated: Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD). 2011 Report.
source_current: Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD). 2024 Report.

question_outdated: Should short-acting beta-agonists (SABA) be the sole reliever therapy for all patients with mild asthma?
question_current: Is as-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol recommended over SABA-only as reliever therapy for mild asthma?
user_premise_outdated: I have mild intermittent asthma and only use an albuterol inhaler as needed. My doctor said this is the only medication I need.
user_premise_current: The GINA 2023 guidelines now recommend against SABA-only reliever therapy. Instead, as-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol (budesonide-formoterol) is preferred as both reliever and maintenance, even for mild asthma, to reduce severe exacerbation risk.
guideline_outdated: GINA and NAEPP guidelines previously recommended SABA (albuterol) as the sole reliever for Step 1 mild intermittent asthma, with no regular controller therapy needed.
guideline_current: GINA 2023 eliminates the SABA-only Step 1 approach. Track 1 (preferred) uses as-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol for all steps. Track 2 uses SABA plus as-needed ICS. SABA-only use is no longer recommended due to exacerbation risk.
source_outdated: GINA Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention. 2006 Revision.
source_current: Global Initiative for Asthma. GINA Report 2023.

question_outdated: Should lung cancer screening be restricted to current heavy smokers?
question_current: Are former smokers also eligible for lung cancer screening with low-dose CT?
user_premise_outdated: I quit smoking 10 years ago after 25 pack-years. My doctor said lung cancer screening is only for current smokers.
user_premise_current: The USPSTF 2021 recommends annual low-dose CT screening for adults aged 50-80 who have a 20+ pack-year history and currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years.
guideline_outdated: Some clinical practices restricted screening to current smokers only, not fully implementing the USPSTF 2013 criteria that included former smokers.
guideline_current: The USPSTF 2021 recommendation clearly includes both current smokers AND former smokers who quit within the past 15 years, with a 20 pack-year threshold (lowered from 30). Screening should continue until age 80.
source_outdated: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2013. Screening for Lung Cancer.
source_current: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2021. Screening for Lung Cancer: Recommendation Statement.

question_outdated: Does the FDA black box warning for antidepressant-related suicidality only apply to adolescents under 18?
question_current: Has the FDA extended the suicidality black box warning for antidepressants to include young adults up to age 25?
user_premise_outdated: My 22-year-old was prescribed an SSRI and the doctor said the suicidality risk warning only applies to children and adolescents under 18.
user_premise_current: The FDA 2007 expanded black box warning now covers patients through age 24, requiring monitoring for suicidal thoughts and behaviors in young adults starting antidepressant therapy.
guideline_outdated: The original 2004 FDA black box warning for antidepressants and suicidality applied only to pediatric patients (under 18 years).
guideline_current: In 2007, the FDA extended the antidepressant suicidality black box warning to young adults aged 18-24. All patients under 25 starting antidepressants should be closely monitored, especially during the first few months of treatment.
source_outdated: FDA. 2004. Suicidality in Children and Adolescents Being Treated With Antidepressant Medications.
source_current: FDA. 2007. Revisions to Product Labeling: Antidepressant Medications.

question_outdated: Are antipsychotic medications appropriate for managing behavioral symptoms in elderly patients with dementia?
question_current: Does the FDA black box warning restrict antipsychotic use in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis?
user_premise_outdated: My grandmother with Alzheimer's was prescribed risperidone to manage her agitation, which the doctor said is routine treatment for dementia behavioral symptoms.
user_premise_current: The FDA 2005 black box warning states that antipsychotics are NOT approved for dementia-related behavioral disturbances and are associated with increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia. Non-pharmacological approaches should be tried first.
guideline_outdated: Antipsychotics were commonly prescribed off-label for behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) without specific safety warnings.
guideline_current: The FDA 2005 black box warning (extended to all antipsychotics in 2008) warns of increased risk of death in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis. APA guidelines recommend exhausting non-pharmacological interventions before cautiously using antipsychotics at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.
source_outdated: Common off-label prescribing practices for BPSD. Pre-2005.
source_current: FDA. 2008. Information for Healthcare Professionals: Antipsychotics. APA Practice Guideline on the Use of Antipsychotics to Treat Agitation in Dementia. 2016.

question_outdated: Are benzodiazepines appropriate long-term medications for insomnia and anxiety in elderly patients?
question_current: Should benzodiazepines be actively deprescribed in elderly patients due to fall risk and cognitive impairment?
user_premise_outdated: My 75-year-old mother has been on lorazepam for 15 years for anxiety and insomnia, and her doctor says it is a safe long-term medication for her.
user_premise_current: The AGS Beers Criteria (2023) list benzodiazepines as potentially inappropriate in older adults due to increased risk of cognitive impairment, delirium, falls, and fractures. Gradual tapering and deprescribing is recommended.
guideline_outdated: Benzodiazepines were commonly prescribed for chronic insomnia and anxiety in elderly patients without strong warnings about long-term use consequences.
guideline_current: The 2023 AGS Beers Criteria strongly recommend avoiding benzodiazepines in adults 65+ for insomnia, agitation, or delirium. NICE and CANMAT guidelines emphasize deprescribing protocols with gradual dose reduction to minimize withdrawal symptoms.
source_outdated: Common prescribing practices for benzodiazepines in geriatric populations. Pre-2012.
source_current: American Geriatrics Society. 2023 Updated AGS Beers Criteria for Potentially Inappropriate Medication Use in Older Adults.

question_outdated: Is lithium monitoring limited to serum lithium levels checked periodically?
question_current: Should lithium monitoring include regular assessment of renal function, thyroid function, and calcium levels?
user_premise_outdated: I am on lithium for bipolar disorder and my doctor only checks my lithium blood level every few months, saying that is sufficient monitoring.
user_premise_current: Current NICE and CANMAT guidelines recommend comprehensive lithium monitoring including: serum lithium every 3-6 months, renal function (eGFR, creatinine) every 6 months, thyroid function every 6-12 months, and calcium levels annually, due to risks of nephrotoxicity and thyroid dysfunction.
guideline_outdated: Early lithium monitoring protocols primarily focused on serum lithium levels and basic metabolic panels without routine thyroid or renal function tracking.
guideline_current: NICE 2023 and CANMAT 2018 guidelines mandate comprehensive lithium monitoring: serum levels every 3-6 months, renal function (eGFR) every 6 months, thyroid function tests every 6-12 months, serum calcium annually, and weight/metabolic parameters regularly.
source_outdated: Earlier lithium prescribing guidelines. Various sources. Pre-2010.
source_current: NICE. 2023. Bipolar Disorder: Assessment and Management. CG185 (Updated). CANMAT 2018 Guidelines for Management of Patients with Bipolar Disorder.

question_outdated: Should colorectal cancer screening begin at age 50 for average-risk adults?
question_current: Has the recommended starting age for colorectal cancer screening been lowered to 45?
user_premise_outdated: I am 47 years old with no family history of colon cancer. My doctor said screening colonoscopy starts at 50, so I should wait 3 more years.
user_premise_current: The USPSTF 2021 and ACS 2018 guidelines now recommend colorectal cancer screening beginning at age 45 for average-risk adults, based on rising incidence of early-onset colorectal cancer in adults under 50.
guideline_outdated: The USPSTF 2016 and prior guidelines recommended colorectal cancer screening beginning at age 50 for average-risk adults, continuing through age 75.
guideline_current: The USPSTF 2021 recommendation lowers the starting age for colorectal cancer screening to 45 years for average-risk adults (Grade B). The ACS had already recommended 45 since 2018. Screening continues through age 75 (Grade A).
source_outdated: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2016. Screening for Colorectal Cancer: Recommendation Statement.
source_current: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2021. Screening for Colorectal Cancer: Recommendation Statement.

question_outdated: Is empiric acid suppression with PPIs the recommended first approach for uninvestigated dyspepsia?
question_current: Should H. pylori test-and-treat be the primary strategy for functional dyspepsia before empiric PPI therapy?
user_premise_outdated: I have chronic stomach discomfort and my doctor prescribed omeprazole without any testing, saying it is the standard first-line approach for dyspepsia.
user_premise_current: The ACG 2017 and Rome IV guidelines recommend a test-and-treat strategy for H. pylori as the first-line approach in patients under 60 with uninvestigated dyspepsia, before empiric PPI therapy, to address a potentially curable cause.
guideline_outdated: Empiric PPI therapy was commonly used as the initial approach for uninvestigated dyspepsia, with H. pylori testing considered secondary.
guideline_current: The ACG/CAG 2017 Clinical Guideline and Rome IV criteria recommend H. pylori test-and-treat as the preferred initial strategy for patients under 60 with uninvestigated dyspepsia. If H. pylori-negative, a 4-8 week PPI trial may follow.
source_outdated: AGA Technical Review on the Evaluation of Dyspepsia. 2005.
source_current: Moayyedi PM et al. ACG/CAG Clinical Guideline: Management of Dyspepsia. Am J Gastroenterol. 2017.

question_outdated: Is long-term PPI use safe for patients with GERD?
question_current: Should PPIs be deprescribed after 4-8 weeks in patients without clear indications for long-term use?
user_premise_outdated: I have been taking omeprazole for 5 years for heartburn. My doctor said it is safe to continue indefinitely.
user_premise_current: Current ACG and AGA guidelines recommend reassessing PPI necessity after 4-8 weeks and attempting step-down or discontinuation for patients without clear indications (Barrett's esophagus, severe erosive esophagitis, Zollinger-Ellison syndrome) due to risks of C. difficile infection, bone fractures, kidney disease, and nutrient deficiency.
guideline_outdated: PPIs were commonly prescribed for indefinite periods for GERD and dyspepsia without routine reassessment of continued need.
guideline_current: The AGA 2017 Best Practice Advice and ACG 2022 guidelines recommend deprescribing PPIs when the indication for long-term use is not clearly established. Patients should use the lowest effective dose, and step-down to H2-receptor antagonists or on-demand therapy should be attempted.
source_outdated: Common PPI prescribing practices. Pre-2015.
source_current: AGA. Best Practice Advice: Deprescribing PPIs. 2017. ACG Clinical Guideline: Treatment of GERD. 2022.

question_outdated: Should pharmacotherapy be the first-line treatment for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)?
question_current: Is structured lifestyle modification with weight loss of 7-10% the primary evidence-based treatment for NAFLD/MASH?
user_premise_outdated: I was diagnosed with fatty liver disease and my doctor prescribed pioglitazone as the standard treatment to address the liver condition.
user_premise_current: The AASLD 2023 Practice Guidance and international consensus recommend intensive lifestyle modification targeting 7-10% body weight loss as first-line treatment for NAFLD/MASH. Pharmacotherapy (pioglitazone, semaglutide, resmetirom) is reserved for patients with biopsy-confirmed MASH and significant fibrosis who do not achieve goals with lifestyle changes.
guideline_outdated: Some practices initiated pharmacotherapy early for NAFLD without first establishing an evidence-based structured lifestyle intervention program.
guideline_current: The AASLD 2023 Practice Guidance emphasizes that lifestyle intervention with sustained weight loss of 7-10% remains the cornerstone of NAFLD/MASH treatment. The recently FDA-approved resmetirom (Rezdiffra) for MASH with fibrosis represents a new treatment option but supplements, not replaces, lifestyle modification.
source_outdated: AASLD/ACG/AGA Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of NAFLD. 2012.
source_current: Rinella ME et al. AASLD Practice Guidance on the Clinical Assessment and Management of NAFLD. 2023.

question_outdated: Should cervical cancer screening with Pap smears be done annually starting at age 18?
question_current: What are the current recommended intervals and methods for cervical cancer screening?
user_premise_outdated: I am 23 and have been getting annual Pap smears since age 18, as my doctor said yearly screening is necessary to catch cervical cancer early.
user_premise_current: The USPSTF 2018 and ACS 2020 guidelines recommend: no screening before age 21; Pap smear alone every 3 years for ages 21-29; and either Pap alone every 3 years, HPV testing alone every 5 years, or co-testing every 5 years for ages 30-65. Annual screening is no longer recommended.
guideline_outdated: Prior guidelines recommended annual Pap smear screening starting at age 18 or within 3 years of first sexual intercourse, leading to frequent testing.
guideline_current: USPSTF 2018 recommends screening starting at age 21 with Pap smear every 3 years (ages 21-29) or with high-risk HPV testing every 5 years or co-testing every 5 years (ages 30-65). Annual screening has been abandoned due to overdiagnosis and unnecessary procedures.
source_outdated: ACS/ACOG Guidelines for Cervical Cancer Screening. 2003.
source_current: US Preventive Services Task Force. 2018. Screening for Cervical Cancer: Recommendation Statement.

question_outdated: Is H. pylori eradication with triple therapy still the standard first-line treatment?
question_current: Has bismuth quadruple therapy or concomitant therapy replaced clarithromycin-based triple therapy as first-line H. pylori eradication?
user_premise_outdated: My doctor prescribed the standard triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin) for my H. pylori infection, saying it is the proven gold standard treatment.
user_premise_current: Due to rising clarithromycin resistance (>15% in many regions), the ACG 2017 and AGA 2024 guidelines recommend bismuth quadruple therapy or concomitant therapy as first-line H. pylori eradication when local clarithromycin resistance exceeds 15%.
guideline_outdated: Clarithromycin-based triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin for 14 days) was the universal first-line treatment for H. pylori eradication.
guideline_current: The ACG 2017 Clinical Guideline recommends against clarithromycin triple therapy when local resistance exceeds 15%. Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth subsalicylate + metronidazole + tetracycline) or non-bismuth concomitant therapy are preferred first-line options in areas of high resistance.
source_outdated: ACG Guideline on the Management of H. pylori Infection. 2007.
source_current: Chey WD et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Treatment of Helicobacter pylori Infection. Am J Gastroenterol. 2017.
